moveAnnotations.annotation: We're opening with the king's pawn, immediately staking a claim in the center and preparing to develop our pieces rapidly. This move controls the crucial d5 and f5 squares while opening diagonals for both our bishop and queen. The move 1.e4 leads to sharp, tactical games where both sides fight for central control from the very first move. As we'll see, our opponent is about to challenge this central pawn in a very provocative way with the Alekhine Defense.

Black immediately attacks our central pawn with the knight, defining the Alekhine Defense – one of the most provocative and double-edged defenses in chess. Instead of matching our central control with moves like 1...e5 or 1...c5, our opponent is inviting us to advance our pawns and gain space while the knight jumps around the board. This hypermodern approach can lead to sharp tactical battles where Black tries to prove that our extended pawn center is more of a weakness than a strength. The knight on f6 also prepares to support a future ...d5 pawn break, which is exactly what's coming next.

We're developing our knight to its ideal square while defending the e4 pawn, but this move also sets up the aggressive Spielmann Gambit variation that's about to unfold. The knight on c3 controls important central squares like d5 and e4, and supports our pawn center while preparing rapid development. This move is more flexible than 2.e5, the main line of the Alekhine, because we're waiting to see how Black continues before committing our pawn structure. However, we're also preparing to meet ...d5 with some very sharp tactical ideas, as we'll see on the next move.

Our opponent strikes at the heart of our position, immediately challenging our central pawn and trying to prove that the early knight development was justified. This central pawn break is the thematic continuation of the Alekhine Defense, putting maximum pressure on our e4 pawn and forcing us to make a critical decision. Black is essentially saying 'I've attacked your e4 pawn with my knight, now I'm attacking it with my pawn too – what are you going to do about it?' The move also opens the c8-bishop's diagonal and gives Black good central control, but it also creates some tactical vulnerabilities that we're about to exploit.

We're advancing our pawn to attack the knight and gain space in the center, forcing Black's pieces into awkward positions. This move kicks the knight away from its strong central post while establishing a powerful pawn on e5 that cramps Black's position and controls key squares like d6 and f6. The advance also opens up the possibility of further pawn advances that could create a strong attack, though it does leave the d5 pawn undefended for now. This is the critical moment where the Spielmann Gambit begins to take shape – we're sacrificing material for rapid development and attacking chances.

Black retreats the attacked knight to d7, which is probably the ideal square since it keeps the knight centralized and doesn't block in the other pieces too badly. From d7, the knight can potentially jump to useful squares like c5 or e5 later in the game, and it also supports the advance of the c-pawn. However, this retreat does allow us to continue with our aggressive plan, and the knight is somewhat passively placed for now. Our opponent is essentially accepting that we'll get a strong initiative in return for the material we're about to sacrifice.

This is the stunning point of the Spielmann Gambit – we're sacrificing our pawn to blast open Black's position and create immediate tactical threats! The pawn advance attacks the f7 square (a traditionally weak point in Black's position) and severely cramps Black's development by controlling key squares in the enemy camp. We're essentially saying that rapid development, open lines, and attacking chances are worth more than the material we're giving up. This move also opens up our light-squared bishop's diagonal and prepares to bring our pieces into the attack with maximum force – the position is about to explode into sharp tactical complications.
